coredata.dc:title: N, S double-doped GQDs intercalated nanoflower-like MoS2 as an efficient counter electrode catalyst for DSSCs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Bixue
Wang, Xuan
Cai, Xu
Yang, Chenzhao
Chen, Mingzhu
Lu, Shuang
Wang, Yinglin
Zhang, Xintong
coredata.dc:description: Designing Pt-free counter electrode (CE) materials with good conductivity, stability and catalytic activity is essential for further development of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). In this paper, MoS2/graphene quantum dots (M/GQD) composites were successfully prepared by inserting GQDs into MoS2 interlayers using one-step hydrothermal method and applied to the CEs of DSSCs. The results of density functional theory (DFT) calculations demonstrated the rationality of the structure of M/GQD, which has a stronger capacity to adsorb I atom. According to the electrochemical results, the M/GQD CEs exhibited excellent catalytic activity and charge transfer capability. The PCE of M/GQD-based DSSCs (5.79 % at 100 mW/cm2 and 18.14 % for simulated indoor light at 1000 lux) was much higher than that of MoS2-based DSSCs (3.11 % and 10.23 %, respectively). The synergistic coupling of M/GQD results in abundant catalytic active sites, excellent conductivity and good stability, creating a promising channel for charge transfer. Overall, our work indicates that the introduction of GQDs provides a facile strategy for synthesizing efficient and stable CE materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dye-sensitized solar cells
Counter electrode
MoS2
Graphene quantum dots
Electrocatalytic activity

We also extend our gratitude to Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for providing technical supporting of characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) MoS2, (b) M/GQD; HR-TEM images of (c, d, e) MoS2, (f, g, h) M/GQD; (i) EDX spectrum of M/GQD; (j–n) the EDX mappings of Mo, S, C, N.
(a) XRD images of MoS2 and M/GQD; FT-IR spectra of (b) MoS2 and (c) M/GQD.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of M/GQD; (b–e) High-resolution XPS spectra of Mo 3d, S 2p, C 1s and N 1s of M/GQD, respectively.
Adsorption energies of I atom on the MoS2 and M/GQD.
(a) CV curves, (b) Tafel polarization plots, and (c) Nyquist plots of different CE catalysts; (d) J-V curves of DSSCs with diverse CEs.
CV curves for 50 cycles at a scanning rate of 50 mV/s and the corresponding relationship between peak current density and cycle time of (a, b) Pt, (c, d) MoS2, (e, f) M/GQD, respectively.
Electrochemical parameters of Pt, MoS2 and M/GQD CEs.
Summary of photovoltaic performance of DSSCs for various CEs.

head.ce:title: N, S double-doped GQDs intercalated nanoflower-like MoS2 as an efficient counter electrode catalyst for DSSCs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science and Technology, Hebei Agricultural University, Huanghua, 061100, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science and Technology, Hebei Agricultural University, Huanghua 061100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of UV Light-Emitting Materials and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, 130024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of UV Light-Emitting Materials and Technology of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of UV Light-Emitting Materials and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing Pt-free counter electrode (CE) materials with good conductivity, stability and catalytic activity is essential for further development of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). In this paper, MoS2/graphene quantum dots (M/GQD) composites were successfully prepared by inserting GQDs into MoS2 interlayers using one-step hydrothermal method and applied to the CEs of DSSCs. The results of density functional theory (DFT) calculations demonstrated the rationality of the structure of M/GQD, which has a stronger capacity to adsorb I atom. According to the electrochemical results, the M/GQD CEs exhibited excellent catalytic activity and charge transfer capability. The PCE of M/GQD-based DSSCs (5.79 % at 100 mW/cm2 and 18.14 % for simulated indoor light at 1000 lux) was much higher than that of MoS2-based DSSCs (3.11 % and 10.23 %, respectively). The synergistic coupling of M/GQD results in abundant catalytic active sites, excellent conductivity and good stability, creating a promising channel for charge transfer. Overall, our work indicates that the introduction of GQDs provides a facile strategy for synthesizing efficient and stable CE materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Clean energy development is an urgent need to address energy and environmental issues, as well as to promote sustainable development. Solar energy stands out among various renewable energy sources due to its cleanliness, durability and availability [1]. Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), as one of the third-generation solar cells, have attracted much attention from researchers due to their environmental friendliness, simple process and strong low-light corresponding capability [2,3]. The counter electrode (CE), as an important component of DSSCs, is mainly responsible for receiving electrons from the external circuit and catalyzing I3 − reduction [4]. Up to now, Pt-based materials have been the most commonly used CE materials due to their excellent electrical conductivity and catalytic activity, but their costliness and scarcity have limited the further development of DSSCs [5]. Finding low-cost Pt-free CEs is critical. Different kinds of materials were investigated to replace the Pt such as oxides [6], nitrides [7], organic polymers [8], transition metal dichalcogenides [9], carbon materials [10], and composites [11], etc. Among them, transition metal dichalcogenides are widely used in CE for their excellent electrochemical properties.

Transition metal sulfur compounds were considered as promising CE materials due to their cheap and easy availability, abundant reserves, high electrocatalytic activity and good stability, especially MoS2 [11]. In particular, MoS2 has excellent electron mobility and a tunable band gap, but unfortunately, its electrical conductivity is poor and the catalytically active sites are concentrate on the edges, with the basal surface being catalytically inert [12,13]. Therefore, various strategies have been tried to improve their properties, such as doping, phase engineering strategies, and preparation of composites, etc [11,14,15]. Due to the inherent catalytic activity and electrical conductivity, the composite of MoS2 with carbon materials has attracted much attention from researchers. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are emerging zero-dimensional carbonous materials with advantages such as good electrical conductivity, a tunable band gap, excellent light stability, and outstanding charge storage ability [16,17]. When GQDs are composited with two-dimensional materials, their quantum effects are important to modulate the charge carrier dynamics for efficient charge transfer as well as carrier separation and collection [18]. In recent years, composites of MoS2 and GQDs have been widely used in various fields, such as hydrogen evolution, sensors, and water pollution treatment applications [18–21], and achieved good results. But, as far as we know, there have been almost no reports of using this composite material for CEs of DSSCs.

In this contribution, we have synthesized MoS2 and MoS2/graphene quantum dots (M/GQD) composites by one-step hydrothermal method, and investigated the effects of hydrothermal reaction time, GQDs contents and film thickness on M/GQD CEs. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations showed that GQDs were successfully inserted into the MoS2 interlayer, and M/GQD had a stronger ability to adsorb I atom. Using them as catalysts for CEs of DSSCs, as expected, the M/GQD CEs exhibited higher J 0 and |J Red1| as well as smaller R ct and E pp, and its photoelectric conversion efficiency (PCE) reached to 5.79 % under standard solar illumination (100 mW/cm2), which is much higher than that of MoS2-based DSSCs (3.11 %). Notably, under low light irradiation (1000 lux), the M/GQD-based DSSCs achieved a high PCE of 18.14 %, and the MoS2-based DSSCs also improved to 10.23 %. The prominent cell performance of M/GQD may be attributed to the compounding effect of MoS2 and GQDs, which produced more electrocatalytic active sites and sufficient fast diffusion pathways of the electrolyte and improved the electrical conductivity, which is beneficial for enhancing catalytic efficacy of MoS2. This work provides a direction for the design of Pt-free CEs with excellent electrocatalytic activity.
The Experimental section including chemicals, sample preparation, fabrication of counter electrodes, and characterizations and measurements can be found in Supplementary materials (S1–S4).

Fig. 1 showed the schematic diagram of the hydrothermal synthesis of MoS2 or M/GQD composites. Using ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate, thiourea and citric acid monohydrate as the precursor, M/GQD composites were produced at 175 °C for 20 h. During the hydrothermal process, ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate was decomposed into Mo sources, citric acid monohydrate was decomposed into C sources, thiourea was decomposed into S source for MoS2 and an N, S doped source for GQDs. As a control, MoS2 was synthesized in the absence of citric acid monohydrate. Notably, due to the co-growth of MoS2 and GQDs during the hydrothermal process, GQDs will be inserted between the MoS2 layers to form M/GQD composites.

Fig. 2 a showed the scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of pure MoS2, which demonstrated a flower-like composite composed of uniform nanosheets. In a comparison, the SEM image of M/GQD in Fig. 2b revealed that after the addition of GQDs, the size of MoS2 decreased, the petal-like structure was gradually dispersed. This suggested that the addition of GQDs could lead to an increase in the specific surface area and improved carrier transport, which in turn improved photovoltaic performance [22]. The energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy (Fig. 2i) demonstrated the presence of Mo, S, C and N and the atomic percentage of Mo to S was close to 1:2, confirming the successful synthesis of the M/GQD. The corresponding elemental mapping (Fig. 2j–n) clearly showed that Mo, S and C elements were uniformly distributed in M/GQD and the presence of the N element indicated the formation of N-doped GQDs. Furthermore, the transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were shown in Fig. 2c–h. As can be seen in Fig. 2c and d, the surface of MoS2 nanosphere was interspersed with tiny nanosheets, which resembled a petal-like structure. In Fig. 2e, the lattice striations of MoS2 were well defined and have a layer spacing of 0.62 nm, which was attributed to the (002) lattice plane of MoS2 [20]. M/GQD (Fig. 2f) indicated that the addition of GQDs preserved the flower-like morphology of MoS2, which agreed with the SEM results. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images in Fig. 2g and h showed that GQDs were successfully modified on the surface petals of MoS2 nanosheets and inserted into the MoS2 interlayer. The lattice spacing of GQDs and MoS2 was 0.23 nm [23,24] and 0.83 nm [25], corresponding to the (1120) lattice plane of GQDs and the (002) lattice plane of MoS2, respectively. The widening of the layer spacing from 0.62 nm to 0.83 nm on the MoS2 (002) crystalline surface may be caused by the insertion of GQDs. The above results confirmed the successful synthesis of M/GQD composites.

The crystal properties of the samples were investigated by XRD, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The diffraction peaks of 13.98°, 28.74°, 32.70°, 35.44°, 43.64°, 57.96° and 69.94° were correspond to the (002), (004), (100), (102), (006), (110) and (108) crystal plane of MoS2 (PDF#37–1492). In the phase card, the lattice spacing of the (002) crystal plane of MoS2 is 0.62 nm, which is consistent with the TEM results. In comparison, the diffraction peaks of M/GQD were significantly weakened, which indicated that the addition of GQDs led to a more dispersed structure of MoS2 and effectively avoided the agglomeration of MoS2 nanoflowers [26], in agreement with the SEM results. At the same time, the XRD peaks of the GQDs were undetected due to its low content [27]. The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was also conducted to further confirmed the formation of MoS2 and M/GQD. As shown in Fig. 3b and c, the two peaks located at 480 cm−1 and 1122 cm−1 belong to the adsorption band of Mo–S groups, while the adsorption peak at 943 cm−1 was attributed to the MoO vibration, which may be due to the presence of a part of MoO3, which is in agreement with the latter XPS results [28,29]. The adsorption peaks near 1035 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 could be attributed to C–O and CO, and the band at 1400 cm−1 belonged to CC [21]. The peaks near 1195 cm−1 and 620 cm−1 correspond to the C–N and C–S stretching vibrations, respectively, which confirmed the doping of N and S on the GQDs [19]. These above SEM, TEM and FTIR results confirmed the successful synthesis of M/GQD and that (N, S-doped) GQDs adsorbed on the MoS2 surface by sharing S atoms with MoS2.

The chemical constituents and structure of M/GQD were further investigated by XPS, as displayed in Fig. 4 . The XPS survey was shown in Fig. 4a, which contained elements such as Mo, S, C, N and O. The Mo 3d spectrum of M/GQD (Fig. 4b) could be characterized by fitting two peaks at 228.92 (Mo 3d5/2) and 232.22 eV (Mo 3d3/2), which were attributed to the Mo4+ species of MoS2 [30,31]. The peaks discovered at 236.58 and 226.20 eV belonged to Mo6+ and S 2s, and the Mo6+ peak may be related to the partial oxidation of the sample [30]. S 2p in Fig. 4c had two main peaks at 161.84 and 163.10 eV, corresponding to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 orbitals, respectively [31]. As indicated in Fig. 4d, the C in M/GQD exhibited four chemical states, which were C–C bond at 284.8 eV [32], C–N bond at 286.3 eV [9], C–OH bond at 287.18 eV [33], C–S bond at 288.64 eV [32]. Among them, the presence of C–S bond implied that GQDs and MoS2 were chemically bonded to each other, and the presence of C–N bond confirmed the doping of N atoms, which was consistent with SEM and FTIR results. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 4e) showed three peaks at 394.90, 398.80 and 401.95 eV, corresponding to Mo 3p, pyridinic-N and graphitic-N, respectively, further confirmed the doping of the N atoms [33]. Overall, the XPS data implied the successful synthesis of the M/GQD. The XPS spectrum of MoS2 is demonstrated in Fig. S1, and its characteristic peaks of Mo and S are basically consistent with the M/GQD.

We used theoretical calculations (DFT) to assess the iodide adsorption on the surface of the electrode material and the calculation methodology was detailed in the Supplementary materials. As shown in Fig. 5 , the GQDs expanded the layer spacing of MoS2 in an intercalated manner and I atom was adsorbed on the surface of the materials. The respective adsorption energies of I atom on MoS2 and M/GQD were −0.56 and −1.88 eV and the respective distances to Mo atoms on the MoS2 and M/GQD surfaces were 4.76 and 4.69 Å. Compared with MoS2, the adsorption energy of I atom on the surface of M/GQD was more negative, indicating that M/GQD exhibited a stronger adsorption capacity for I atom [34,35], which was favorable to promote the reduction reaction of I3 − on the surface of the CEs, and thus enhance the catalytic performance of the material.

In order to determine the optimal preparation conditions for MoS2 and M/GQD, Tafel, EIS and CV (50 mV/s) tests were performed, and the results were shown in Figs. S2–S4. When the reaction time was 20 h, the best catalytic performance was obtained for pure MoS2 CEs. And the M/GQD CEs showed the best catalytic performance when the concentration of GQD was n = 0.5 and the number of scrapings was 9 times.

The catalytic activity of the optimized samples was investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) at 50 mV/s (Fig. 6 a). All curves show two pairs of redox peaks, where the redox peak on the left side corresponds to the I3 −/I− reaction and the redox peak on the right side corresponds to the I2/I3 − reaction [36]. In the study of counter electrodes, the redox peak on the left side plays a crucial role in the catalytic performance of the electrodes, providing information on the cathode peak current density (J Red1) and peak-to-peak separation (E pp). Larger |J Red1| and smaller E pp tend to imply higher catalytic activity [3]. As can be seen from the CV plots of Pt, MoS2 and M/GQD CEs (Table 1 ), |J Red1| is Pt (1.66 mA/cm2) > M/GQD (1.62 mA/cm2) > MoS2 (1.44 mA/cm2) from large to small, and E pp is Pt (0.40 V) < M/GQD (0.46 V) < MoS2 (0.52 V) from small to large. Compared with MoS2 CEs, M/GQD CEs had higher |J Red1| and smaller E pp, indicating that the redox reaction on M/GQD surface could proceed rapidly, and dye sensitizer could be regenerated smoothly, thus it had superior electrocatalytic activity. This may be due to the fact that the incorporation of GQDs increased the specific surface area of the material, thereby exposing more active sites, and facilitated electron transfer.

To investigate the redox reactions on the surface of CEs, CV curves were recorded at different scan speeds. As shown in Fig. S5, with the increase of scanning rate, the positions of the oxidation and reduction peaks were shifted toward positive and negative potentials, respectively, and the peak current densities showed a good linear relationship with the square root of the scanning rate, which suggested that the redox reactions at the interface of the electrolyte/CEs were mainly affected by the diffusion-controlled mechanism, and that there were no specific interactions between the redox pairs and the CEs [37].

Fig. 6b shows the Tafel polarization curves of Pt, MoS2 and M/GQD CEs, which can be roughly divided into the polarization zone (low potential zone), the Tafel zone (medium potential zone) and the diffusion zone (high potential zone) [38]. The exchange current density (J 0), and the limiting diffusion current density (J lim) can be obtained from the Tafel curve, where J 0 is represented by the value of the longitudinal coordinate corresponding to the intersection of the tangent line made at −0.12 V and the central axis. They are closely related to the reduction ability of the CEs for I3 − in the electrolyte. The magnitude of J 0 and J lim are determined by equation (1) and equation (2):
(1)
J 0
=
R T
n F
R
c t
(2)
J lim
=
2 n F D c
l
where R, T, n, F, R ct, D, c and l are the gas constant, absolute temperature, the number of electrons transferred for the reduction of I3 −, Faraday's constant, charge transfer resistance, diffusion coefficient, concentration of I3 − and electrode spacing. The larger J 0 and J lim represented the better the catalytic performance. As shown in Table 1, M/GQD CEs had much higher values of J 0 and J lim than MoS2 CEs, indicating that the prepared M/GQD CEs had faster reaction kinetics. The data were consistent with the results of the CV curves.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the prepared samples was also carried out and the results were shown in Fig. 6c and Table 1. Typically, the resulting Nyquist diagrams have two semicircles, the left semicircle corresponds to the interfacial charge transfer resistance R ct, whose intersection with the horizontal axis corresponds to the series resistance R s, and the right semicircle corresponds to the Nernst diffusion impedance ZW [39]. In the prepared samples, the values of R ct were Pt (1.92 Ω) < M/GQD (4.52 Ω) < MoS2 (39.59 Ω) in ascending order, indicating the higher electrochemical activity. In short, the EIS results showed that M/GQD had an improved catalytic activity and was close to that of Pt, which was agreed with CV and Tafel results. The enhanced performance is attributed to that the incorporation of GQDs enhanced the conductivity and reduced the charge transfer resistance of MoS2, and its unique structure also provided a new channel for electron transport.

In order to gain insight into the photovoltaic performance of the counter electrode materials, the photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) curves were carried out for different CEs at 100 mW/cm2 (AM 1.5G). The photovoltaic performance parameters such as power conversion efficiency (PCE), fill factor (FF), short-circuit current density (J sc), and open-circuit voltage (V oc) can be derived from the J-V curve, as shown in Table 2 . From Fig. 6d and Table 2, it can be seen that the PCE of the M/GQD-based DSSCs is 5.79 % (V oc = 0.67 V, J sc = 13.42 mA/cm2, FF = 0.64), which is an improvement of 86 % compared to the PCE of MoS2-based DSSCs (3.11 %, V oc = 0.56 V, J sc = 8.53 mA/cm2, FF = 0.65). This suggested that M/GQD was an advanced CE catalyst, which may be related to the fact that the incorporation of GQDs enhanced the conductivity of the material, increased the number of active sites, and facilitated electron transport. In addition, there is still a gap between the PCE of M/GQD-based DSSCs and that of Pt-based DSSCs (7.56 %), which may be attributed to the poor adherence of the CE films prepared by the scratch-coating method to the substrate surface, which increases the series resistance of the device, thus substantially reducing the FF of the device and resulting in a lower cell performance.

In order to further confirm the contribution of the addition of GQDs to the performance enhancement of DSSCs, we further tested the J-V curves of the devices under simulated indoor illumination (1000 lux LED), as shown in Fig. S6 and Table S1. It can be found that the PCE of M/GQD-based DSSCs (18.14 %) is enhanced by 77 % compared to MoS2-based DSSCs (10.23 %) under simulated indoor light, which further proves the enhancement of MoS2-based catalysts by GQDs and indicates the direction of indoor PV development.

In summary, M/GQD did have superior electrochemical and photovoltaic properties, which was consistent with the DFT calculations. We also proposed the possible catalytic mechanism of M/GQD CE towards the I3 − reduction. First, the insertion of GQDs enlarged the layer spacing of MoS2, which provided more adsorption sites for I3 − reduction. Second, the synergistic effect of the enlarged layer spacing of MoS2 and the excellent electrical conductivity of GQDs provided the channels for charge transport and reduced its transport resistance. On top of all that, the stronger adsorption of I atom on M/GQD could accelerate the reduction reaction of I3 −, and thus exhibited more excellent material properties.

Excellent electrochemical stability played an important role in improving the performance of the device. Successive 50 circles CV curves measurements (50 mV/s) were performed to examine the stability and reversibility of different CEs, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . After 50 CV cycles, the cathode (anode) peak current densities of Pt, MoS2 and M/GQD CEs decreased to 95.47 % (97.75 %), 80.23 % (63.66 %), and 83.02 % (71.27 %) of the initial ones, respectively, indicating that the addition of GQDs improved the stability. I-t curves (Fig. S7) was further tested at −0.4 V, the stability of the M/GQD CEs improved by 132.65 % compared with that of MoS2 CEs, which further confirmed that the incorporation of GQDs had an indelible effect on the stability of MoS2 CEs.

In summary, M/GQD composites were successfully prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method, and the introduction of GQDs was effective in improving electrocatalytic performance and stability. The incorporation of GQDs not only changed the morphology of MoS2 and increased the number of active sites, but also enhanced its electrical conductivity and promoted charge transport. DFT calculations showed that the insertion of GQDs effectively enlarged the layer spacing of MoS2 and enhanced its adsorption energy for I atom. Under optimized conditions, M/GQD CEs exhibited higher J 0 (3.24 mA/cm2) and lower R ct (4.52 Ω) compared to MoS2 CEs (J 0 = 0.51 mA/cm2, R ct = 39.59 Ω). DSSCs prepared with M/GQD CEs can have a PCE as high as 5.79 % (18.14 % under simulated indoor light), which was much higher than that of MoS2-based DSSCs (PCE = 3.11 %, 10.23 % under simulated indoor light). This research suggested that M/GQD composites could be a cost-effective and Pt-free alternative for the further development of DSSCs and other electrochemical applications.

Bixue Sun: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xuan Wang: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xu Cai: Investigation, Data curation. Chenzhao Yang: Investigation, Data curation. Mingzhu Chen: Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Shuang Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yinglin Wang: Resources, Funding acquisition. Xintong Zhang: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support of the Basic Scientific Research Funds for Universities Affiliated to Hebei Province of China: KY2023019; the Special Program for Talent Introduction of Hebei Agricultural University: YJ201928; the Open Foundation of Key Laboratory for UV-Emitting Materials and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Normal University: 135131009; the Jilin Scientific and Technological Development Program: 20230101110JC and the Natural Science Foundation of China: 62074031. We also extend our gratitude to Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for providing technical supporting of characterization.

J. Colloid Interface Sci.
